facts_disclosed: Yoga three mornings a week in Coral Gables

turns.content: I need a good recommendation for a post-workout smoothie. Something that isn't going to taste like grass clippings.

I can definitely help with that. A few questions first: are you looking for something more on the protein/recovery side or more of a refreshing fruit-based smoothie? And any dietary restrictions or ingredients you absolutely won't tolerate?

Recovery, I think. I do yoga three mornings a week at a studio in Coral Gables and by the time I get home I'm starving but I don't want a heavy breakfast. Something I can make quickly before I have to get to the courthouse.

Yoga three mornings a week plus a job at a courthouse -- sounds like you've got a tight morning window. Here's a recovery smoothie that takes about three minutes and will hold you until lunch without feeling heavy:

**Tropical Recovery Smoothie:**
- 1 cup frozen mango chunks
- 1/2 banana (fresh or frozen)
- 1 scoop vanilla protein powder
- 1 tablespoon almond butter
- 1 cup coconut water
- Small handful of spinach (you genuinely cannot taste it with the mango)

Blend until smooth. The mango and almond butter give it a creamy, almost dessert-like quality, and the coconut water rehydrates without adding heaviness.

If you want to prep ahead, you can bag the frozen fruit and spinach in individual portions on Sunday and just dump a bag in the blender each morning. Cuts the time down to about 90 seconds.
Now see, you say I won't taste the spinach but people always say that and then there's a definite... greenness to it. I'm a judge, I notice when people aren't being entirely forthcoming.

Fair point, and I respect the cross-examination. I'll concede: with spinach there is sometimes a faint earthy undertone, even if you can't visually identify it under all that mango.

If you want zero risk of greenness, skip the spinach entirely. The smoothie works fine without it. Or substitute with a tablespoon of ground flaxseed -- it adds fiber and omega-3s without any detectable flavor. It just slightly thickens the texture.

Alternatively, if you want the nutrients without the taste gamble, you could do a greens powder capsule on the side. All the virtue, none of the flavor compromise.

The capsule idea is very practical. I like that. Now, I've been doing this yoga for about two years and at my age -- I'm fifty-five -- flexibility has become more important than anything else. My instructor says hydration and nutrition after class make a real difference in how the body recovers. What else should I be thinking about?

Your instructor is right, and at 55 with a consistent yoga practice, you're doing one of the best things possible for long-term mobility. A few things that support flexibility and recovery specifically:

**Hydration:**
- Electrolytes matter more than just water volume, especially after yoga where you're sweating. Coconut water (already in the smoothie) is solid. You could also add a pinch of sea salt and a squeeze of lime to regular water -- cheap, effective.

**Nutrition timing:**
- Eating within 45 minutes of finishing class makes a measurable difference in muscle recovery. Your smoothie plan covers this perfectly.
- Magnesium is worth paying attention to -- it supports muscle relaxation and flexibility. Many people over 50 are mildly deficient. A magnesium glycinate supplement before bed can help with both recovery and sleep quality.

**Movement on off days:**
- On the days you're not at the studio, even 10 minutes of gentle stretching keeps the connective tissue supple. The gains from three classes a week hold better if the body doesn't go completely cold on the other four days.

Are you doing any other exercise besides the yoga, or is that your primary movement?

That is my primary movement. Although, the magnesium -- I have heard of that before. A friend of mine swears by it for sleep. Is there a difference between the different types? Because I went to the supplement aisle once and it was overwhelming. Felt like I needed a pharmacology degree.

The supplement aisle is genuinely confusing, and that's partly by design -- companies love creating variations. Here's the simple version:

- **Magnesium glycinate** -- best for sleep and muscle relaxation. Gentle on the stomach. This is the one I'd recommend for your purposes.
- **Magnesium citrate** -- good for general supplementation, also helps with digestion. Can have a mild laxative effect at higher doses.
- **Magnesium oxide** -- cheap and common but poorly absorbed. Skip this one.
- **Magnesium threonate** -- marketed for cognitive function, more expensive, less evidence.

For you, glycinate is the clear winner. Take 200-400mg about an hour before bed. Start at the lower dose and see how you feel. Most people notice better sleep within a few days, and over time it supports the muscle recovery from your yoga practice.

One brand note: look for one that's third-party tested -- the label will say USP, NSF, or ConsumerLab verified. The supplement industry isn't well regulated, so that third-party stamp is your quality assurance.

Third-party tested. Good. I appreciate evidence-based recommendations, not marketing. Now, Carlos -- that's my husband -- keeps trying to get me to walk with him in the evenings but by the time I get home from work I am not in the mood for much beyond sitting down with a glass of wine. Maybe I should take him up on it.

Honestly, yes. Evening walks with your husband would complement the yoga beautifully -- yoga handles flexibility and balance, walking handles cardiovascular health and joint mobility. And it doesn't have to be a power walk or a commitment. Even 20 minutes at a comfortable pace counts.

Plus, the mental shift from courthouse to home is real. A walk can serve as a buffer between work mode and evening mode. Some people find it does more for decompression than the wine does -- though I'm not here to take your wine away.

Maybe try it twice a week to start and see if it feels like a chore or a relief. If Carlos is already asking, he'll probably be thrilled.

He would be thrilled. That man has been asking me for months. Claro, he's right and I know it, but admitting that to him is a different matter entirely. Let me ask you something else. On the days I do yoga, my lower back feels wonderful. On the days I do not, by three in the afternoon I can feel the tightness coming back. Is that normal?

Completely normal, and it's actually a sign that the yoga is doing exactly what it should. What's happening is that your yoga practice is mobilizing the joints and lengthening the muscles around your lumbar spine and hips. But those benefits are temporary if the connective tissue isn't being maintained between sessions.

Sitting for long periods -- which I imagine your job requires -- compresses the lumbar spine and tightens the hip flexors. By 3 PM, you've been seated for hours and the gains from your morning practice have effectively worn off.

Two simple things you can do at the courthouse:

- **Stand and do a gentle forward fold** for thirty seconds every hour or so. You don't need a yoga mat -- just hinge at the hips, let your arms hang, and breathe. It decompresses the lower back almost instantly.
- **A seated spinal twist** in your chair -- feet flat, rotate your torso and hold for a few breaths each side. Takes fifteen seconds and nobody needs to know you're doing it.

These aren't replacements for yoga, but they bridge the gap on off days.
A forward fold in chambers. I can do that. I am not doing it in open court. My bailiff would have questions.

Fair enough -- the seated twist is your courtroom option. Nobody would even notice. The forward fold is for chambers, during a recess, or in your office with the door closed. Judicial dignity fully preserved.

The bigger point is that your body is telling you the yoga works -- it just wants more consistency throughout the day. Even those small movements signal to your muscles and joints that they shouldn't tighten back up.

That makes sense. So the overall plan -- yoga mornings, the smoothie, stretching on off days, magnesium at night, and maybe walking with Carlos. Simple, no nonsense. And the smoothie bags prepped on Sunday -- that is the kind of practical advice I was looking for.

That's the whole picture, and none of it requires a major lifestyle overhaul. You're already doing the hardest part -- showing up at the studio three mornings a week consistently. Everything else is just supporting what you've already built.

So your morning plan: yoga at the Coral Gables studio, come home, blend the tropical smoothie minus the spinach, greens capsule on the side, out the door. On off days, a short stretch. A quick forward fold or seated twist at the courthouse. Magnesium glycinate before bed. And maybe -- on your own terms and timeline -- an evening walk or two with Carlos.
Thank you. I will start with the smoothie this weekend and go from there. And I will not tell Carlos the walks were your idea.

I wouldn't dream of taking credit. Enjoy the smoothie, and I hope the yoga studio in Coral Gables continues to serve you well. Your body will thank you for the consistency -- flexibility at 55 is an investment that pays off every single year going forward.
